chunk_summary: Management of undernutrition in children with neurodisabilities requires individualized nutrition plans, feeding safety assessments, and early intervention to prevent complications like aspiration and caregiver burnout.
learning_objectives: Understand the unique nutritional challenges in children with neurodisabilities such as CP.
Learn to assess feeding safety and modify feeding practices to reduce aspiration risk.
Recognize the importance of individualized energy and protein prescriptions based on tone and mobility.
Identify when to escalate to supplemental tube feeding and utilize local resources like RBSK and DEIC.
key_facts: Feeding safety screen should be conducted at every visit, focusing on coughing, choking, wet voice, or meals lasting >30 minutes.
Energy needs vary based on tone and mobility; reassess frequently to ensure adequacy.
Functional indicators like feeding time, choking episodes, and respiratory infections should complement anthropometry in growth assessment.
Constipation exacerbates appetite and reflux; treat aggressively to improve feeding outcomes.
Use energy-dense home foods (e.g., oil/ghee in porridge, nut pastes, dairy) to support weight gain when needed.
Delaying supplemental tube feeding can worsen respiratory morbidity and caregiver burnout.
reference_quote: Start with a feeding safety screen at every visit: coughing or choking with feeds, wet voice, recurrent pneumonia, or meals consistently lasting >30 minutes should prompt referral for feeding assessment and texture modification rather than 'more calories.'
document_key_topics: Role of early intervention and caregiver support
